data.title: Japan Offering Y4.5T Of TD-Bills (No 1295)
data.description:
TOKYO--Japan's Ministry of Finance said Friday it is auctioning Y4.5 trillion of treasury discount bills.

 
Issue                  TD-Bill (Number 1295) 
Settlement Date                 Mar 24, 2025 
Maturity Date                   Jun 23, 2025 
Offer Amount                   Y4.5 Trillion

data.title: Air New Zealand Buys Back 631,121 Ordinary Shares
data.description:
Air New Zealand repurchased 631,121 ordinary shares for cancelation as part of its buyback program disclosed on Feb. 20, according to a Friday filing with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

The acquisition comprised 311,867 ordinary shares bought via the NZX at an average price of NZ$0.6152 and 91,153 ordinary shares at an average price of AU$0.5617 on the ASX. It also acquired off-market 321,871 ordinary shares from the Ministry of Finance for NZ$0.6148 apiece.

Company shares rose nearly 3% in recent Friday trade on the New Zealand bourse.

data.title: Hong Kong Inflation Slows to 1.4% in February
data.description:
Hong Kong's consumer prices rose 1.4% year over year in February 2025, a slower increase compared with the 2.0% recorded in January, according to data released on Thursday by the Census and Statistics Department.

The smaller increase in February was mainly due to the high base of comparison from February 2024, when the Chinese New Year contributed to higher food prices and package tour charges, the department said.

Net of government relief measures, underlying inflation stood at 1.1% in February, down from 1.6% in January.

Taking the first two months of 2025 together to smooth out the impact of the Chinese New Year, consumer prices increased 1.7% year over year, while underlying inflation was 1.3%.

By category, prices of alcoholic drinks and tobacco surged 20.9%, utilities jumped 13.3%, transport prices rose 2.6%, housing costs increased 1.6%, while miscellaneous goods, outside meals, and takeaway food all saw a 1.2% rise, the department said.

On the other hand, basic food prices decreased by 1.9%, clothing and footwear by 1.9%, and durable goods by 1.1% year over year.

A government spokesman noted that underlying consumer price inflation remained moderate in the first quarter of 2025.

data.title: Singapore Shares Fall 0.3%, Tracking Wall Street's Losses — Market Talk
data.description: Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index falls 0.3% to 3918.21, tracking Wall Street's losses overnight. U.S. trade policy uncertainty has continued to weigh on sentiment, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a research report. The U.S.' tariff exemption on selected Canadian and Mexican imports expires on April 2 and the U.S.' reciprocal tariffs on its trading partners are expected to be announced on the same date, the analysts add. Among the worst performers on the benchmark index, Singapore Technologies Engineering falls 2.0%, Jardine Matheson Holdings drops 1.0%, and Singtel is 0.9% lower. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Iron Ore Capped Unless China Steel Demand Improves — Market Talk
data.description: There's likely to be a lid on iron-ore prices without a meaningful improvement in China's domestic steel demand, Citi reckons. The bank sets its 0-3-month forecast at $100/metric ton, highlighting limited supply disruptions in Australia and Brazil, and tipping the pace of supply growth to accelerate. A recent reduction in port stocks and pickup in crude steel output have, among other factors, kept prices from falling below that mark, it says. "Potential supply-side reform of the China steel sector has been top-of-mind for investors with consensus expectations of circa 50 million tons of output cuts this year," Citi says. All else being equal, a cut of that size would be bearish for iron ore, it says. Spot iron ore rose by 0.1% Thursday to $101.75/ton, according to S&P Global Commodity Insights. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: South Korea Producer Prices Rise 1.5% in February
data.description:
Producer prices in South Korea increased 1.5% in February from a year earlier, according to preliminary data from the Bank of Korea on Friday.

The on-year gains were primarily driven by price increases across manufacturing products, electric power, gas, water and waste, and services, which rose 1.3%, 5.1%, and 1.6%, respectively, the preliminary data indicated.

The prices for agricultural foods, livestock products, and marine foods declined 2.1% last month.

On a month-on-month basis, producer prices were unchanged in February, according to the data.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 9 PM ET
data.description:
U.S. Stocks End Lower After Optimism Over Rate Cuts Wanes

The post-Fed rally sputtered Thursday, with major U.S. stock indexes ending lower after an up-and-down session.

Japan Consumer Inflation Slowed in February

Japan's consumer inflation slowed in February thanks to the government's reintroduction of energy subsidies but remained well above the Bank of Japan's target of 2%.

Bank of Canada Must Stop Tariff-Fueled Price Increases From Spreading, Macklem Says

The remarks are likely to reinforce the market's view that the central bank is headed for a pause in April stemming from a hot inflation reading.

Turkey's Central Bank Raises Overnight Lending Rate in Unscheduled Meeting

The move comes after the arrest of Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, the top political rival to President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

U.S. Treasury Sanctions Network Supporting Iranian Oil Exports

The sanctions are part of the administration's "campaign of maximum pressure on Iran," the department said.

France to Put Extra $1.85 Billion Into Defense via Public Investment

State-owned financial institutions will invest the funds in the country's defense companies as European countries prepare for a shakeup of the continent's security order.

South African Reserve Bank Holds Main Repo Rate at 7.5% Amid Rising Uncertainty

The South African Reserve Bank held its main repo rate at 7.5% on Thursday, as tensions with the U.S. continue to rise amid escalating global uncertainty.

U.S. jobless claims still showed few private-sector layoffs, but more ex-federal workers got benefits

The private sector is still culling very few jobs, the latest report on jobless claims shows, but more federal workers fired by the Trump administration are applying for benefits.

Homes Sales Unexpectedly Rose in February

Sales of existing homes rose 4.2%, reflecting an increase in shopping activity and in the number of homes for sale since the start of the year.

Global Central Banks Wrestle With Slowing Growth, Stubborn Inflation

The Bank of England kept interest rates steady, as major economies confront a nasty mix of faltering economic growth, rising inflation and tariff threats.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 9 PM ET
data.description:
FedEx Cuts Outlook, Despite Higher Profit, Sales

FedEx cut its outlook for the year, citing weak demand for business-to-business shipments stemming from continued weakness and uncertainty in America's industrial economy.

Nike Forecast Steeper Fourth-Quarter Sales Drop in Early Days of Turnaround

Nike expects its sales decline to accelerate as it continues to push discounts to clear inventory in the hopes of stoking a turnaround.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang Apologizes for His Quantum Effect

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang offered a quantum-level climb-down on Thursday, after spooking quantum-computing companies earlier this year with his assessment that their efforts wouldn't be "very useful" for 15 to 30 years.

Energy Capital's Partnership With Abu Dhabi's ADQ Also Seen Profiting KKR

A $25 billion investment agreement with the ADQ sovereign-wealth fund will also lift the private-equity firm's $50 billion collaboration with KKR, ECP's founder says.

Boeing Sued for Wrongful Death by Family of Whistleblower

Lawsuit by family of John Barnett blames jet maker for 2024 suicide; Boeing says it is saddened by his death.

Ben & Jerry's Founders Say They Stand Behind Ousted CEO

The executive's dismissal is latest dust-up in a yearslong conflict between the brand and its parent company, Unilever.

Tesla Recalls Most Cybertrucks

More than 46,000 of the electric vehicles are being recalled over the potential for an exterior panel to fall off.

Accenture Begins to Feel the Trump Crackdown on Consultants

GSA has asked federal agencies to justify which consulting contracts are mission critical.

Disney's New 'Snow White' Movie Is Making Pretty Much Everyone Mad

The live-action remake of the studio's beloved classic managed to hit every possible cultural land mine on its journey to theaters

Temu Owner PDD Posts Slowest Revenue Growth Since Early 2022

The Chinese owner of bargain app Temu reported slower quarterly profit and revenue growth, capping a turbulent year for the e-commerce giant as it faced stiff competition at home, geopolitical tensions abroad and U.S. tariff uncertainties.

data.title: CZR Resources receives $75m offer for Robe Mesa project as bidding war intensifies
data.description: CZR Resources has received a conditional $75 million proposal from three major international iron ore companies for its Robe Mesa project in Western Australia. The unsolicited proposal has been lodged by the Robe River JV, made up of Rio Tinto (53%), Mitsui & Co (33%) and Nippon Steel Corporation (14%). If the offer is completed, CZR would retain its interests in other projects such as the Croydon gold project, the Buddadoo project and its 50% interest in the Ashburton Link project. Bidding war underway The bid for Robe Mesa comes a day after CZR received an unsolicited proposal from Gold Valley Iron Ore to acquire all CZR shares via an on-market takeover bid. On the same day, Fenix Resources announced it had received acceptances from shareholders for 10.04% of all CZR shares in response to the conditional off-market takeover bid it had launched for the company in February. The CZR board has so far determined that the Gold Valley proposal does not constitute a superior offer compared to the binding offer from Fenix. Fenix offer favoured The CZR board continues to recommend the Fenix offer, with directors unanimously in favour of the proposal in the absence of a superior one. The Robe Mesa deposit is CZR’s cornerstone project with ore reserves of 33.4 million tonnes at 55.0% iron and a resource base of 45.2Mt at 56% iron. The project’s low strip ratio provides a low-capex direct shipping ore opportunity with a rapid, low-cost path to production. Debt funding consideration As part of its efforts to obtain the support of major shareholders, the Robe River JV would consider providing CZR with an equivalent amount of debt funding to that offered under the working capital loan provided by Fenix, along with a further amount equal to the break fee under the bid implementation agreement between CZR and Fenix Resources. After consultation with its external legal advisers, the CZR board has determined that the Robe River approach is a potential competing offer that could reasonably be expected to lead to a superior proposal. As such, the CZR board intends to engage with Robe River JV in relation to its proposal.

data.title: Trump Rescinds Order Targeting Law Firm Paul Weiss — WSJ
data.description:
By Erin Mulvaney

President Trump on Thursday rescinded an executive order targeting Paul Weiss, as one of the nation's top law firms bent to White House pressure and agreed to a series of concessions to avoid a client exodus.

The agreement between Paul Weiss and the White House includes a commitment by the firm to provide $40 million in pro bono legal services to support the administration's initiatives, such as assisting veterans and fighting antisemitism.

Trump agreed to lift the order after meeting with the firm's chair Brad Karp, who worked behind the scenes to mitigate the fallout for Paul Weiss following the president's sanctions against the firm last Friday.

Trump's executive order had directed federal agencies to remove any of Paul Weiss's government security clearances, restrict its access to federal buildings and to strip government contracts from companies it represents.

The president alleged the firm used its position to undermine the judicial process and criticized an array of its work, including a lawsuit brought by the D.C. attorney general's office against individuals who stormed the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021. Trump also cited the firm's ties to Mark Pomerantz, who had a two-decadelong association with the firm before going to work on an investigation at the Manhattan district attorney's office into Trump and his business.

The White House on Thursday said Karp acknowledged "the grave dangers of Weaponization, and the vital need to restore our System of Justice."

"We look forward to an engaged and constructive relationship with the President and his Administration," Karp said.

Paul Weiss, whose clients include some of the largest companies on Wall Street, was the third major firm targeted by Trump. The president has pledged that more sanctions against the world of "Big Law" are coming.

Write to Erin Mulvaney at erin.mulvaney@wsj.com

data.title: ANZ Group Holdings Launches Account Lock Down Feature
data.description:
ANZ Group Holdings launched Digital Padlock, a feature that allows customers to lock down access to their accounts if they suspect cybercriminals are targeting them, the financial services company said in a statement Thursday.

The feature, which will be available in the ANZ App, ANZ Plus, and Internet Banking from the middle of the year, will disable digital access, stop unauthorized activity, block credit and debit cards, and notify ANZ's fraud monitoring system, Falcon, the statement said.

The Digital Padlock will not impact customers' future-dated payments, like loan repayments or direct debits, according to the statement.

data.title: Top Glove's 3Q Earnings Could Be Weaker on Softer Demand — Market Talk
data.description: Top Glove could report sequentially weaker earnings for fiscal 3Q due to lower glove demand following front-loading by U.S. buyers ahead of higher tariffs on China-made gloves, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. Increased competition in non-U.S. markets may also pressure average selling prices, he reckons. However, demand recovery is anticipated from 4Q as U.S. clients exhaust their stockpiles, he says. Aw cuts his FY 2025-FY 2027 EPS estimates for Top Glove by 6.1%-19.9% to factor in slower increases in selling prices and sales volume in 2H amid competition from China. CIMB Securities lowers Top Glove's target price to MYR0.95 from MYR1.40 while maintaining a hold rating on the stock. Shares are 1.7% higher at MYR0.91. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Austin Metals Begins Drill Program at Western Australia Project; Shares Surge 20%
data.description:
Austin Metals has begun a reverse circulation drilling program at the Austin gold project in Western Australia, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The program will comprise an initial 10 to 15 holes for around 1,500 meters and will target several high-priority targets identified from historic and recent drilling, per the filing.

The mineral explorer's shares surged 20% in recent Friday trade.
